findings.ai_reason: Behavior: this PowerShell command downloads a ZIP from %ZIP_URL% with Invoke-WebRequest and extracts/copies its contents into the project directory. Why: downloading and executing/extracting remote content is a real supply-chain risk because the script trusts an external archive and moves its files into the repo root. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri '%ZIP_URL%' -OutFile $zipFile and Expand-Archive -Path $zipFile -DestinationPath $extractDir -Force, then Copy-Item ... -Destination $projectRoot -Recurse -Force.

Behavior: this PowerShell command fetches the latest Node.js LTS MSI from nodejs.org and saves it locally. Why: it performs a network download of an installer, which is a risky action even if the host is a legitimate vendor because it introduces external dependency and installer execution flow. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -OutFile '%NODE_INSTALLER%' -UseBasicParsing -ErrorAction Stop.

Behavior: this script downloads the Node.js LTS index JSON from nodejs.org to determine the latest version. Why: it is a direct outbound web request, which is the exact suspicious behavior flagged here; in this installer context it is used for version discovery and is not inherently malicious, but it is still a network fetch. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri 'https://nodejs.org/dist/index.json' -UseBasicParsing | ConvertFrom-Json.

Behavior: this script downloads a Node.js MSI installer from a URL built from the fetched version and writes it to a temp path. Why: it is a remote download of an executable installer, which is a sensitive action and can be abused if the source or version selection is compromised. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -OutFile $installerPath -UseBasicParsing -ErrorAction Stop.

Behavior: this comment documents a curl command piped directly into bash. Why: piping remote content into a shell is a classic code-execution pattern and is risky because the downloaded script runs immediately. Evidence: curl -s https://bitbucket.org/daam2251/ai-agent-marketplace/raw/main/scripts/install-windows.bat | bash.

Behavior: this PowerShell command downloads auto-start-windows.bat from BASE_URL into a temp file. Why: it is a remote fetch of a script that will later be executed, which is a supply-chain risk in an installer flow. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri '%BASE_URL%/auto-start-windows.bat' -OutFile '%SCRIPT_FILE%' -UseBasicParsing -ErrorAction Stop.

Behavior: this usage comment shows invoking install.ps1 via irm from a remote URL. Why: it downloads a script over the network for immediate execution, which is the risky behavior flagged. Evidence: irm https://bitbucket.org/daam2251/ai-agent-marketplace/raw/main/scripts/install.ps1 | iex.

Behavior: this usage comment shows piping a remote PowerShell script into Invoke-Expression. Why: this is direct remote code execution from a downloaded script and is inherently risky. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri https://bitbucket.org/daam2251/ai-agent-marketplace/raw/main/scripts/install.ps1 | Invoke-Expression.

Behavior: this script downloads install-windows.bat from BASE_URL to a local file. Why: it fetches a script from the network that is intended to be executed, which is a supply-chain risk. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "$BASE_URL/install-windows.bat" -OutFile $SCRIPT_FILE -UseBasicParsing -ErrorAction Stop.

Behavior: this usage comment shows curl piping a remote install.sh directly into bash. Why: this is immediate execution of downloaded code and is a well-known risky pattern. Evidence: curl -s https://bitbucket.org/daam2251/ai-agent-marketplace/raw/main/scripts/install.sh | bash.

Behavior: this usage comment shows curl piping a remote install.sh directly into sh. Why: it executes downloaded shell code immediately, which is risky by design. Evidence: curl -s https://bitbucket.org/daam2251/ai-agent-marketplace/raw/main/scripts/install.sh | sh.

Behavior: this comment shows curl piping the nvm install script into bash. Why: it downloads and executes remote shell code, which is a risky execution pattern. Evidence: curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.3/install.sh | bash.

Behavior: this comment repeats the same curl-to-bash nvm installation command. Why: it is the same remote-script execution risk as above. Evidence: curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.3/install.sh | bash.

Behavior: this imports child_process.execSync in a Node setup script. Why: in a repo with setup/install tooling, importing child_process is normal and not itself a vulnerability; no malicious command is shown by the import alone. Evidence: import { execSync } from 'child_process';.

Behavior: this helper runs an arbitrary command string via execSync with shell: true. Why: this can be dangerous if untrusted input reaches runCommand, but the provided snippet does not show any call sites or tainted inputs, so exploitability cannot be determined statically from the evidence shown. Evidence: execSync(command, { cwd, stdio: 'inherit', shell: true }).

Behavior: this executes 'node --version' via execSync to check the installed Node version. Why: in a setup script this is a normal environment check and the command is fixed, not user-controlled. Evidence: execSync('node --version', { encoding: 'utf-8' }).

Behavior: this checks for window.ethereum in a frontend web3 service. Why: using the Ethereum provider object is expected in a dApp and is not evidence of exfiltration or wallet theft by itself. Evidence: typeof window.ethereum !== 'undefined'.

Behavior: this requests accounts from the injected Ethereum provider. Why: eth_requestAccounts is standard MetaMask connection flow in a web3 frontend and is normal for this repo type. Evidence: window.ethereum.request({ method: 'eth_requestAccounts' }).

Behavior: this creates an ethers BrowserProvider from window.ethereum and gets a signer. Why: this is standard wallet/provider setup for a dApp and does not itself read or leak wallet data. Evidence: new ethers.BrowserProvider(window.ethereum) and provider.getSigner().

Behavior: this queries eth_accounts from the Ethereum provider. Why: reading the connected account list is normal wallet state access in a frontend dApp and not evidence of exfiltration. Evidence: window.ethereum.request({ method: 'eth_accounts' }).

Behavior: this lazily initializes the provider and signer if an account exists. Why: this is normal synchronization logic for a web3 frontend and does not indicate malicious wallet access. Evidence: new ethers.BrowserProvider(window.ethereum) and signer = await provider.getSigner().

Behavior: this requests wallet_switchEthereumChain on the injected provider. Why: chain switching is a standard dApp interaction and is expected in web3 apps. Evidence: window.ethereum.request({ method: 'wallet_switchEthereumChain', params: [{ chainId: `0x${chainId.toString(16)}` }] }).

Behavior: this registers an accountsChanged event listener on window.ethereum. Why: listening for wallet account changes is normal frontend web3 behavior and not evidence of data theft. Evidence: window.ethereum.on('accountsChanged', callback).

Behavior: this removes the accountsChanged listener from window.ethereum. Why: cleanup of an event listener is normal and benign. Evidence: window.ethereum.removeListener('accountsChanged', callback).

Behavior: this registers a chainChanged event listener on window.ethereum. Why: tracking network changes is standard dApp behavior and not inherently suspicious. Evidence: window.ethereum.on('chainChanged', callback).
Behavior: this removes the chainChanged listener from window.ethereum. Why: listener cleanup is normal and benign. Evidence: window.ethereum.removeListener('chainChanged', callback).
